Hi @emlys , this solution looks great to me! Thanks for thinking hard about the float edge case. It's definitely something we can put in the issue queue if we want to. It didn't sink in until after our call that the case you might be most worried about is if someone *thinks* they have integer-like values (e.g. 3.0) but they end up on the wrong side of a `numpy.digitize` bin. I was more fixated on the fact that no one would reasonably ask to reclassify a number like 3.00000000001, so we shouldn't design for it. 

Anyway I think it's good to get this clear fix in like you have it, and deal with the rest another time if needed.

I glanced at the tests we already have for `reclassify_raster` and I think they're adequate to cover these changes. I noticed we actually do test with a float raster, which seems appropriate.

Impressive work! It's surprising that this functionality could be added in such little code. Although, I'd like for us to think carefully about whether it makes sense to modify the ``ParamConeProg`` and ``Dcp2Cone`` classes or subclass them. Here are some thoughts:

* I'm not a fan of changing the ``ParamConeProg`` class so that it allows nonlinear objectives. I'd prefer to define a new class called ``ParamQuadConeProg`` that subclasses ``ParamConeProg``. If it seems like doing so would require a lot of code duplication then I think that's an argument to simplify some of the functions in the ``ParamConeProg`` class to handle smaller units of functionality a piece.

* I also think that ``Dcp2Cone`` should be clearer with its scope as to affine versus quadratic representations. I can see how defining a new canonicalization class might be overkill, but perhaps a name change is in order.

There is also a helper for which I've requested additional documentation.

Take the following as a comment only, by someone who previously fought with another stepper motor controller with a 400 page manual (:D), not(!) a request to remodel. I fully understand if you consider it "good enough" and just want to get this in.

In general, I feel a bit confused by the naming and API: slewing and moving relatively are methods, positions are a property; there is `slew` and `go`, which sounds pretty similar effect-wise; the sometimes slim distinction between step-the-verb (as in the method of that name) and step-the-attribute (in various args) and "step-the-noun" (in the property `steps`) vs. "position" vs `step_position`;
I think it could be clearer to have a triplet of methods like `move` (slew), `move_for` (steps, in direction), `move_to` (a position), but I understand why you want a `position` property to query. maybe add a thin wrapper method for the latter? 
Also, should `step` and `go` be `_step` and `_go`, i.e. "private" properties, to reduce the API surface a bit? Or do you envision yourself using this a lot, e.g. in preprogrammed but delayed moves?

I have resisted this before, however my position has moved from "against" to "neutral".  I think we do want this for the ophyd v2 work (it is basically a new project), we want to do that work in the same tree (because it is not actually a new project and we are going to make sure they inter-operate), and formatting the whole code base is going to be way less hassle than trying to only format _some_ files.

One of my big concerns with blanket formatting is that this is in a very real sense un-reviewable (+10k/-8k LoC) and I have seen formatting tools put bugs in.  However black is widely enough used and checks the consistency at the AST level so I am confident that the reformatting will be correct.

LGTM. Let me summarize the understanding that I've gathered as of now (this might be obvious to some readers).

1. Returning `NotImplemented` from a binary operation (instead of raising `NotImplementedError`) is a signal that a certain operation is not implemented, but a fallback may be available (e.g., if `a.__eq__(b)` is not implemented, `b.__eq__(a)` might still be available). Only if all recovery attempts fail, an exception is being raised.
2. `scipy_wrapper` ensures that all binary operations fall back to the cvxpy implementation when `other` is a cvxpy expression.
3. This might not always be necessary, though, as SciPy may already provide this signal in their implementation, e.g., [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/8e55aea98ee0a89e1404acec410aeb879aef33a7/scipy/sparse/_base.py#L678).

As discussed in a call with @alexrashed, we're both not super happy with the second part of the PR. Fundamentally it's about preserving the host header (other proxies don't do this by default btw, it has to be enabled).

I think there's a cleaner solution without re-using the `SERVER` field in this way. Given that the `SERVER` field refers to the server socket that accepted the request, it fundamentally doesn't make sense in a client context. I think it would be better to accept the fact that an HTTP client API that uses server-side constructs, needs additional client-side arguments to work correctly, and extend the API accordingly. In this particular case, we need to be able to tell the `requests` call what the target server should be. If not otherwise specified, we can still use the `Host` header (which still makes sense in a client context).

Even though we're using the word "server" in both cases, fundamentally the `SERVER` attribute in the WSGI environment refers to a server-side concept (which socket accepted the request), whereas the "server" in the case of the client API refers to a client-side construct (where to send the request to).

Then, when calling the HTTP client in `Proxy.forward` class, we use the host part of the `forward_base_url` to pass to the `server` attribute.

Here's a patch to demonstrate. Admittedly it's not so nice that we break the contract for the `Proxy.request` implementation, but that could easily be worked around when needed, and it's kind of a a gimmick in the first place. https://github.com/localstack/localstack/commit/95524532286ca9ae2cf281cad5b54b19e1118c51

Let me know what you think.

Looks pretty nice. Good job.

I had a couple small suggestions and then I have one big one that I would like tested out either in this PR or a promise that it will be done in a future PR. Right now the general flow of the dask arrays goes like this:

```
data -> delayed(_sort_weights) -> indexing and linspace -> delayed(_get_bin_statistics) -> reshape
```

To me there isn't much benefit to having that middle indexing and linspace section of code. The only benefit I see is if the indexing is much faster when done in parallel with dask, but since the indexes are coming from a delayed function (no real chunking) and it is then fed into the second delayed function right away. I think all this logic should be one giant delayed function. It'd be nice if it could be done with `map_blocks` but I don't see a way to do that. The only reason not to do this would be if combining all of these resulted in a large memory usage because all of the temporary arrays (`del idxs_sorted` might help with that).

Lastly, while looking at how `self.idxs` is generated, I think that entire function (`_get_indices`) should be turned into a map_blocks call. There are a lot of little intermediate operations there that are just adding more overhead to the dask graph. However, now that I say that I see that `y_idxs`, `x_idxs`, and `idxs` are all assigned to `self` in this method so maybe it isn't as pretty as I had hoped. Wait! No, `y_idxs` and `x_idxs` are never used outside this method, right? Having a `map_blocks` function that took `lons` and `lats` as inputs and returned the equivalent of `self.idxs` should clean up the dask graph a bit and I would hope improve memory usage and maybe execution time. Bare minimum the dask graph would look better.

Let's remove the dependency on OpenCV and add some more robust tests that showcase more precisely the issue. I've pointed out how we can do it.

Also, so that others can know more precisely what was the issue, can you change the description of the PR to describe what was the original issue and how we fixed it?
There were two problems:
- we were reading 8 bit pixels and copying those as a single 8bit pixel in the output (which was fixed with the `png_set_expand_gray_1_2_4_to_8`
- we were using the number of bytes of a row to skip the number of pixels in the output tensor. Given that a row could have non-integer number of bytes (e.g., 105 bits in a row for 105 1-bit elements), there would be rounding that would appear and thus it's more robust to use the number of elements directly, given that we assume that the output is always `uint8`.

About cherry-picking this fix into the release, we should check with the Nikita but I think it's too late for it now. This was a bug that was already present before (so not a new breakage), but we can make sure to cherry-pick it to the dot release (if we have it).

We supported "in place" operations in a previous version of freud, but I am hesitant to re-introduce in-place operations. I think parameters like this introduce API confusion and inconsistency with _every other method_ that could possibly operate in place but hasn't been enabled. It means we have to double (or at least augment) our testing code, worry about whether data is read-only (we just closed #424), keep more documentation up to date, etc. and the benefits are small (avoiding one extra copy of data). I'm open to others' thoughts on this but as a maintainer I am unsure about reneging on our changes in the freud v2 design.

Thanks @jinran-google. It makes sense. Overall the code LGTM. I just added few smaller suggestions below.

But thinking more about this, I have an idea/suggestion for a rather different overall approach which I'd like to bring up.

- I understand the main goal/reason that we don't want to reset the entire chip if there are multiple radio (BLE, etc) on it when Thread starts.
- The solution in this PR is to add mechnasim to just reset the radio.
- But we are leaving all other platform modules that OT may use and the `otInstance` itself to continue to run as before (e.g. `Alarm/Timer` platform, or UART, etc.).  I guess this is fine and works for RCP (for now).

What we really want is that when we get a reset command (from host) to not reset the whole chip. We really want to have all the platform modules reset and then reinit the `otInstance` (clear all RAM variable) and start running `otInstance` fresh.

I think we can realize this differently:
- From `Instance::Reset()` we call `otPlatReset()` which we currently assume would reset the whole chip.
- We can change the implementation of `otPlatReset()` in platform layer to not trigger chip reset, but instead it can reset all the plaform modules (Radio, alaram, etc). 
- In OT code, we change the `Instance::Reset()` such that after calling `otPlatReset()` we call `Finalize()` immediately followed by `InitSingle()` call again (to reinit the `otInstance`). 
  - If the `otPlatReset()` does actually reset the chip, then we won't get to the `Finalize()` and `InitSingle()` callls (which is fine).
  - If the `otPlatReset()` resets all the platform components/modules, then we are reinitializing the `otInstance` to start fresh (which clears all RAM variables). 

I think this can work. The main advantages I see is that it resets all components without resetting the chip. It delegates the reseting of platform modules to the platform implementation itself which can reset all platform modules (radio, timer, any other platform module we may use in future)... It avoids the need for us to add mechanism in OT To handle reset of one platform components (like reseting radio only - which can be tricky to do).

Thoughts?

A few additional thoughts besides the comments in the review.
About registration of functions, it is not unusual in some web contexts (for example routing in [Flask](https://flask.palletsprojects.com/en/1.1.x/quickstart/#routing)).
Usually this permits to have more control on the functions to be exposed, compared to using `all` and separate modules.

When comparing decorators and a mapping dictionary, in this context I prefer decorators because it looks cleaner to me. Moreover, using a `feature` decorator allows to enforce some validation, or other pre or post processing, in a similar way to what it would be possible subclassing a base abstract class.

Anyway using decorators is a personal preference, and some web frameworks allows to use either approach. For example in [aiohttp](https://docs.aiohttp.org/en/stable/web_quickstart.html#run-a-simple-web-server) (`app.add_routes([web.get('/', hello)])` vs `@routes.get('/')`)

@rynkk, thanks for this. Especially as this is one of the more convoluted areas in the code.
As suggested by @studioj I believe this batch size needs to be set at a class instance level. i.e. in here something like DEFAULT_BATCH_SIZE
https://github.com/pycontribs/jira/blob/678903805e34d91c3905d73d20fcc7a3f5c8036c/jira/client.py#L343

I completely understand where you are coming from with wanting to set this per function. It provides the most flexibility, especially as you will likely have more issues than dashboards and therefore would want to set it differently for different resources. Also scales differently depending on how fast the server is you are working with.

A solution I would prefer if you strongly would like to set this per endpoint might look like so:

```python
# within the DEFAULT_OPTIONS

# A dict of (Resource Type, Batch Size) 
DEFAULT_BATCH_SIZE: Dict[Resource, int] = {
    Issue : 500,
    Resource: 100,
}


...

# inside the JIRA.__init__

if not default_batch_sizes.get(Resource):
    _LOG.warning() # warn that the user will be defaulting to our default
    default_batch_sizes[Resource] = 50 # or some arbitrary
self.options["default_batch_size"] = default_batch_sizes  # or whatever the argument name is



...
# now inside fetch pages

        if maxResults:
            page_params["maxResults"] = maxResults
        else: # will need to set batch size to get all resources
            page_params["maxResults"] = self.options["default_batch_size"].get(item_type, self.options["default_batch_size"].get(Resource))
            _LOG.verbose(f"Batch size for {item_type.__name__} set to {page_params["maxResults"]}")
```

There might be problems with this approach too, but I feel it may be more ergonomic for advanced users such as yourself without confusing/bogging down more casual users

A few years later, some review comments (I'm mostly looking at this from the POV of the Signer interface and  #447):

I think we should not expose all of this as a new module -- especially things like `get_keys_on_hsm()`, `get_hsms()` seem like things that should not be public API here

`HSMSigner` on the other hand sounds like a reasonable thing: The API would include 
* key and device ids as constructor arguments (optional if PyKCS supports that)
* PKCS module loaded using environment variable (optional if there are reasonable defaults for some systems)
* PIN as constructor variable

This would be compatible with #447 with a URI like `hsm:device/0/slot/c9` or `hsm:?slot=c9&device=0`, and pin getting requested with `secrets_handler()`
 
After implementing HSMSigner, the remaining public functionality in this PR would then be `export_pubkey()`. Interestingly this same issue comes up with KMS keys... Maybe _public key export_ could actually be a method on the Signer as well: not all implementations would support it but some could?

Another thing that comes up with KMS keys as well is the need to prehash the payload. We should add that support somewhere -- figuring out the algorithm from the public key is currently very annoying. Can we have `Key.get_payload_hash_algorithm()`?

I'll leave it to others to actually review this in terms of whether they want this behavior and via the action or not.  But, I do like this form and have used it for quite awhile.  It basically shifts the actions jobs 'required checks' project configuration into the source so it can be kept in sync as new Python versions are added and old removed.  No transitional period nor extra admin actions needed.

It has become a bit more complicated "recently" due to changes in GitHub actions and how they handle dependent jobs.  That change was accounted for in PRs such as https://github.com/twisted/towncrier/pull/392.  I also looked at creating an action and a reusable workflow and while they are useful, the way all this data works does force you to define the `if:` here at the usage site as well as passing that along through a `toJSON()` call.  So it's a bit less tidy than I'd like, but I also don't have a recommendation for a cleaner form than provided here in terms of the job itself.

Cool! Nice approach that can further streamline the CORS UX. 🎉  

Was able to replicate with the following steps:

1. create a CloudFront distribution, as per our [docs](https://docs.localstack.cloud/user-guide/aws/cloudfront/):
```
$ awslocal s3 mb s3://test
$ echo 'Hello World' > /tmp/hello.txt
$ awslocal s3 cp /tmp/hello.txt s3://test/hello.txt --acl public-read

$ domain=$(awslocal cloudfront create-distribution --origin-domain-name test.s3.amazonaws.com | jq -r '.Distribution.DomainName')
$ echo $domain
ce6554a5.cloudfront.localhost.localstack.cloud
```

2. create a simple `index.html` file that connects to the enpoint:
```
<html>
<script>
fetch('http://ce6554a5.cloudfront.localhost.localstack.cloud:4566/hello.txt');
</script>
</html>
```

3. upload the file to a bucket and expose it as an S3 website:
```
awslocal s3 mb s3://test
awslocal s3 cp /tmp/index.html s3://test/index.html --content-type text/html
awslocal s3api put-bucket-website --bucket test --website-configuration 'ErrorDocument={Key=index.html},IndexDocument={Suffix=index.html}'
```

4. open http://test.s3-website.localhost.localstack.cloud:4566/index.html in the browser, observe the Network console (request to `ce6554a5.cloudfront.localhost.localstack.cloud:4566`)

Result:
* returns a `403` error on current master  🟥 
* returns 200 with the changes in this PR  ✅ 

---
To me, this achieves a decent balance between improving convenience (works out of the box without extra configs), while not sacrificing security (requests still only allowed from `*.localhost.localstack.cloud` pages in the browser). 👍 As usual with CORS, would be good to get a second pair of eyes on it, though.. 👀 /cc @dfangl @alexrashed @thrau

> I used `range` to fix the issue, because of the way the condition is imposed. If `enumerate` is used, pre-commit will complain of not using `first_value_loc` in the loop body. One code-tidying rule would have in one way ("Use `enumerate` if you use both the index and the value!") and another code-tidying rule would have it the other way ("Use `_` if you don't reference the index in the loop!") I guess the rules are in conflict here because using `break` to intentionally get the first index value that satisfies some condition and use it beyond the loop itself is breaking the abstraction of what a loop is for. (But it's fine to use it here because this is imperative programming; `break` to find the first index satisfying a condition is a common pattern.) We talked about this on Zoom and I remember the context; this is the right fix. I'll merge it now because it looks like a simple PR, and it looks done.

It's more than a code-cleanliness/DRY thing (although that is important): when working on the specialized JSON parser last week, I found that Windows _randomly_ segfaulted when two classes in different parts of the codebase had the same name.
